# Cheat Sheet — advisor-product-strategy

The 15 heuristics needed in most conversations, ordered by expected frequency of use. Each links to its pack — load the pack for the full claim, boundary conditions, and counters before betting anything big on a one-liner.

1. End decision-bearing founder comms with an assertion + explicit ask — "I need Y by Z, risk is C, I'll own the mitigation" — never a bare status, rarely an option menu. (→ influence-and-the-case)
2. Price the argument in the founder's currency — revenue, speed, customer conviction; craft rationale is noise on that channel, lead with the business consequence. (→ influence-and-the-case)
3. Classify the door before building the case: two-way doors get a fast ask at ~70% info with the reversal condition named; only one-way doors earn the memo. (→ influence-and-the-case)
4. Within-noise score gaps (1.7 vs 1.3) are debate topics, not rankings — never execute the sorted RICE list. (→ prioritization-on-thin-evidence)
5. Only validation that costs the prospect something counts — cash, calendar, reputation; meeting enthusiasm is politeness data, and a paid pilot only counts with pass/fail acceptance criteria. (→ prioritization-on-thin-evidence)
6. Win it before the room: pre-wire the 2–3 people who can kill it 3–5 days out, collect dissent privately before positions go public, walk out with the decision written. (→ influence-and-the-case)
7. Push back only when you hold new external data; anxiety pivots get ~48h to land; deference to the HiPPO chosen deliberately is a portfolio decision — log it in the ledger. (→ influence-and-the-case)
8. Write kill criteria as state+date in the brief before starting, and attack the monkey before the pedestal — mid-flight you is compromised by sunk cost. (→ prioritization-on-thin-evidence)
9. First-run, no-results, and error states are launch-blockers — each says what happened + what to do next; populated-view polish can ship never. (→ ux-for-technical-products)
10. Name the severity rung; only red-route failures block — ship cosmetic-only releases, with a scored debt ticket that gets real sprint capacity. (→ ux-for-technical-products)
11. Build-to-close needs all three gates at once — real contract money (1yr+), already on the 24-month roadmap, a named second customer — priced at fully-loaded cost, not deal-revenue-vs-build-cost. (→ prioritization-on-thin-evidence)
12. The demo that closed the deal gets a planned funeral — hand off the learning, never the code. (→ prioritization-on-thin-evidence)
13. 5 users buys one iteration of loud problems on one homogeneous group — 3×5 with fixes beats 1×15; go to 10 when a miss is expensive. (→ research-on-a-budget)
14. Score discovery by decisions changed and what participants spent (time/reputation/money) — "sounds great, keep me posted" is a soft no. (→ research-on-a-budget)
15. AI edits ship the full loop — editable plan before, diff-plus-reason after, edit-in-place draft state, apply/revert inside existing undo — risk-tier the gates, never auto-apply irreversibles. (→ ux-for-technical-products)
